title: Candidate pairwise query-conditioned interaction

queries: query conditioned label token pooling candidate scoring scalable multiclass ranking
sample conditioned verbalizer token attention multiclass candidate scorer

evidence.why_now: EXP129 showed that pure pairwise interaction restores throughput but loses too much salt-side signal, while EXP138 showed that improving only the static label encoder with position-aware pooling is still too weak. EXP099 remains the strongest evidence that candidate-token information matters. The next justified scalable step is therefore to keep the pairwise scorer but make label-token aggregation explicitly sample-conditioned, so candidate-token evidence is selected relative to the current prompt without reintroducing autoregressive rollout.

hypothesis: If the missing scalable signal is not token order alone but prompt-conditioned selection over candidate tokens, then replacing static label pooling with a sample-conditioned token attention pool inside the pairwise scorer should recover part of the salt-side candidate-token benefit while keeping the multiclass path in a non-autoregressive O(L + C*T) regime that remains plausible for Stage 4 pretraining.
notes: Keep Stage 3 finetune-only; do not introduce pretrain, loss-family changes, sampling changes, or packaging changes.
This family keeps the fast pairwise scorer but replaces static candidate-view pooling with prompt-conditioned token attention over class-id and raw-label candidate text.
The intended benefit is to recover some of the useful candidate-token selectivity from the hybrid line without returning to autoregressive candidate rollout.
Launched on 2026-05-09 through the local Stage 3 launcher.
Completed on 2026-05-09 with global verdict: failed.
user-churn improved strongly, user-ltv remained inside the replay-aware neutral band, and item-incoterms stayed far below the strict MRR baseline.
Conclusion: sample-conditioned single-vector label pooling is still too weak; the active frontier should move to token-level candidate statistics rather than more pooled-label near-neighbors.
